preFix.title: The Feather Marbling Pattern
preFix.subtitle: Single-tooth draws from a nonpareil base to create feathers, snails, and peacock tails
preFix.excerpt: The feather and its variations, including the snail and peacock tail, are produced by drawing a single comb tooth through a nonpareil base in a repeating zigzag. The result is among the most dramatic of classical marbled patterns and a natural progression after the nonpareil is mastered.
preFix.sourceNotes: Halfer, Josef. Progress of the Marbling Art. English translation. Buffalo, NY: O.H. Neumann, 1893/1894. Woolnough, C.W. The Art of Marbling. London: J. Simmons, 1881 expanded edition.

attrs.body: Alum is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying the mordant and rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan is non-toxic but spilled size is slippery; wipe spills immediately. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.
attrs.heading: Marbling chemistry safety

content.text: The pattern hierarchy
Classical marbling patterns build on each other. The stone provides the raw field of colour. The nonpareil rakes that field into tight waves. The feather, snail, and peacock tail all start from a
nonpareil base
and add further manipulation. Each step can be stopped and printed as a finished result, or continued to the next level of complexity.

The feather is usually the first pattern to try after the nonpareil. The tool needed is simple, a single comb tooth or stylus point, and the pass itself is easy to follow. The result, though, looks far more complex than the nonpareil.
Setting up the nonpareil base

Prepare the carrageenan size, mordant the paper, and complete a full nonpareil pattern as described in the nonpareil technique entry. The nonpareil must be complete: both rake passes (wide first, fine at right angles) done, and the surface covered evenly with tight waves.

For feather work, the nonpareil wave spacing matters. A finer nonpareil (more teeth per centimetre in the second rake) produces smaller, tighter feather barbs. A wider nonpareil produces bold, open feathers. Both are correct; choose based on the intended scale of the pattern.
The single-tooth draw
Use a single tooth from the comb, or a pointed stylus. You will make a series of diagonal draws across the nonpareil surface.

Position the tooth at the top-left corner of the tray, touching the size surface. Draw diagonally down and to the right, moving toward the bottom-right corner at roughly 45 degrees. As the tooth moves through the nonpareil, it pulls the wave pattern sideways. The lines on one side of the draw bend toward the direction of travel; the lines on the other side bend away. This asymmetry is what produces the feather barb shape.

Lift the tooth from the surface. Reposition it slightly to the right of the previous start position (the distance between feather barbs is the distance between starting positions). Draw diagonally down and to the left this time, from top-right toward bottom-left.
Keep alternating direction across the full width of the tray. The result is a set of diagonal draws that together make the
feather pattern
: a repeating V-shape where the waves bend in opposite directions, like a large feather or fishbone.
Speed and pressure

The tooth should barely break the size surface: think of it as resting on the surface rather than ploughing through it. Too much depth tears the colour pattern rather than pulling it. The speed of the draw affects how far the waves travel before returning to their original direction: a faster draw produces longer, more pronounced barbs; a slower draw produces shorter, more condensed ones.
Maintain consistent speed throughout each diagonal draw. Accelerating or decelerating mid-draw changes the barb length at that point, which disrupts the regular rhythm of the finished pattern.
The snail and peacock tail variations
The
snail pattern

is produced by making feather draws from both long sides of the tray simultaneously, moving toward each other. The two sets of opposing draws meet near the centre, producing a scrolling ripple that Halfer described as a snail. The practical challenge is keeping both passes at matching speed and angle.

A simpler version: complete the feather on half the tray from one side, then complete the feather on the other half from the opposite direction. The meeting line in the centre of the tray will show the snail effect where the two sets of draws interact.

The peacock tail, described by Woolnough in 1881, adds a final pass with a wide-spaced comb drawn vertically through a finished feather pattern. The vertical pass pulls the feather barbs into a gentle bow shape, resembling the eyespots on a peacock's tail feathers. The risk is over-working the pattern: the peacock pass must be a single clean movement, not repeated.
Common problems

items.fix: Reduce depth so the tooth barely touches the surface. The asymmetry of the barb is what creates the feather; if both sides pull equally, the result is a chevron rather than a feather.
items.cause: Tooth going too deep and pulling symmetrically through both sides
items.symptom: Barbs are the same length on both sides of each draw

items.fix: Stick to a single set of feather draws; never go back and re-draw. Use a coarser nonpareil base for the next attempt.
items.cause: Too many passes over the same area or nonpareil base was too fine to show the feather clearly
items.symptom: Pattern looks blurred rather than crisp

items.fix: Check size viscosity; increase draw speed slightly.
items.cause: Size too thin, or tooth moving too slowly
items.symptom: Colour pattern tears or drags rather than bending
